data.title: Various Assets Extend Their Hot Streaks - Can It Continue? (You Might Be Surprised)
data.description:
Welcome back, readers and loyal subscribers. Glad you are with us. We hope that you had a good week and were able to profit from the various markets that continued to move higher.

The continued move higher

Stocks finished the week broadly higher after a mid-week pullback. The rally in stocks continues, as we will illustrate below, with new all-time highs this past week in the Dow and the S&P 500. The S&P was up 0.86% for the week.

The S&P 500 has risen for six consecutive weeks and nine of the past ten. @CyclesFan points out the S&P 500 has had six other six-week winning streaks in the past decade. The seventh week has been a coin toss, with three instances closing higher and three closing lower.

The NASDAQ scored a fresh three-month high and ended the week up 0.22%. To the market’s surprise, semiconductors had a negative week as we describe in the Big View bullets below. But it was the small-cap stocks and the Russell 2000 that stole the show this past week, up 1.98%. See IWM charts below:

Most surprisingly, some of the other asset classes performed even better than stocks. We will go into more detail shortly.

Global easing

The European Central Bank (ECB) delivered its first back-to-back rate cuts in well over a decade in the wake of several cooler-than-expected European inflation reports. This helped propel the US dollar to gain strength as the US economy continues to show strength despite the global growth worries that keep resurfacing (additional charts are forthcoming).

A strong earnings season is upon us. Recession fears abate

Since the beginning of the year, it seems that we have heard nonstop from the doom and gloom market predictors that corporate earnings would begin to decline and that we would eventually feel the shock (major sell-off) of their grim prognostications. They have been proven wrong time and again by a resilient and growing US economy.

Given that this is an election year, coupled with a Fed easing campaign, high stock and corporate bond liquidity, low defaults on high-yield paper and lower-trending inflation, it is not surprising that most asset classes (ex the energy sector) continue to find bullish buyers and surprisingly big beats on corporate earnings. (More on lagging sectors shortly.)

This past week, the banks (and financials) came alive, as most of the early big banks not only had good earnings but beat by a wide margin, well above top line revenues as well as net earnings per share.

With equity multiples a bit stretched right now and corporate bond spreads tightening, many investors are beginning to use options and other hedge instruments to protect their gains going into the election season and end-of-year rebalancing.

Yet, many investors, including our own Mish, recently extolled the 2-year birthday of the new bull market, and if historians are correct, we may still be in the early innings of the “bull market” which could run for a few more years.

My conservative nature says that the markets should be concerned given the high levels of debt, lack of interest by foreign entities to invest in our US Treasury market, an aging population, and the onset of automation. We could be in for some turbulent and more volatile markets in 2025 and beyond.

Continued soft landing?

The latest Bank of America Global Fund Manager Survey shows the odds that a future (2025) soft landing may have slightly decreased. But the hard landing respondents faded just as much, falling into the single digits for the first time since June, with just 8% seeing a recession in the next 12 months. See chart below.

A great 12 months for the equity markets

Would it surprise you to know that since October 1, 2023, the S&P 500 is up 37% and the NASDAQ 100 (QQQ) is up 38%? I recall attending an investment conference last October and many of the stock publishers as well as investment managers sharing their thoughts that we could see a bumpy road in 2024, although all seemed to agree that it was an election year and that might help the markets stay buoyant.

Several of the market experts I listened to or met with at the conference also came out with negative market concern this past summer, convinced that we might not see the markets go any higher than 5500 by year-end. Several issued warnings to “get out” (in case you are not aware, the S&P is near 6,000 as of last Friday’s close).

We are fortunate at MarketGauge to have several risk-managed investment strategies that have stayed invested for most of 2024. Several of these have participated in most of the market’s upside.

It really has been a good 12 months. See the chart below on the S&P 500:

I have read many articles on why the few mega-cap S&P 500 stocks (the “Mag 7”) are fueling the market. The following chart shows that this may no longer hold true. The stock market has broadened considerably.

Additionally, the market breadth of the NYSE, as measured by the advance/decline line representing the issues that keep advancing versus declining, shows healthy, broad, and fully participating stocks listed on the NYSE. These are generally large-cap market leaders, including financial stocks. At the moment, this chart shows no divergences and ammunition on why the market could easily move higher. See chart below:

A few important areas of the market (S&P 500) have not been participating, until lately

There is not a week that goes by that I don’t read several economic writers and analysts suggest that the market is “stretched and a bit frothy”. While this may be true for a few sectors (technology, industrials, utilities to name a few), there are a host of “risk-on” sectors that are not demonstrating the same kind of exuberance in earnings and price movement as yet. Perhaps these will awaken over the next few months.

They are all important members of Mish’s Economic Modern Family and it will be interesting to see if they rotate into a leadership role, especially Transportation stocks, which, believe it or not, have not confirmed the “Dow Theory” as of yet. See charts below:

It’s mostly about earnings

In several past Market Outlook columns, we have cited several reasons the market continues to move higher. To recap what we have stated in the past, we offer the following points:

  1. Lower-trending inflation;
  2. US monetary policy, which has created excess liquidity, is also being derived from the current administration’s bills, which have extended trillions in corporate and union pension fund help;
  3. Federal Reserve interest rate cut; and most importantly,
  4. Higher corporate profitability and earnings (some as a direct result of the past 3-year inflation spike and being able to raise their prices). These surprise earnings beats have been able to support higher stock multiples and stock prices.

Likewise, many analysts believe that corporations have taken advantage of pricing power, and that, on its own, has contributed to a big part of the inflation we have experienced over the past few years. The following chart illustrates this:

Profits vs. inflation. "Rising corporate profits have been a key driver of US inflation, according to Bloomberg."

Can good earnings continue? The earnings for the mega-cap Magnificent 7 should be well above expectations for the most recent quarter, as was witnessed by Netflix (NFLX) this past week. However, analysts also are suggesting that earnings expectations may disappoint in 2025 for all but the largest companies (Magnificent 7). See below:

Mag 7 vs. Other 493. The Mag 7 are expected to report earnings growth of 18.1% YoY in Q3, compared to just 0.1% growth for the Other 493. This gap is expected to narrow in the quarters that follow.

Other asset classes remain on a hot streak

This week’s column is about various assets (and market cap areas as well) that have been on bullish runs recently as well as most of the year. It might surprise you to know that while the stock market has done very well for 12 months, the following alternative asset classes have actually performed better (since October 1st, 2023):

We illustrate these moves by the metals in the following charts.

Gold is on track for its best year since 2007. It has quietly outperformed stocks, up +30% YTD, while the S&P 500 is +23%. On Friday, gold made a fresh all-time high.

This week also saw a breakout in silver and silver mining stocks, which bodes well for the metal going forward. See charts below:

Is this a surprise? Not to us!

First off, our own market and economic guru Mish has been pounding the table since the summer of 2023 for investors to buy gold and silver along with their respective miners. We have numerous recorded appearances by Mish on National TV on shows like Fox Business with Charles Payne where she told him (and the audience) that it was time to load up on the shiny metals during late 2023 and early 2024.

Some of the reasons Mish and our algos have been invested in the metals are these facts: inflation remains elevated, the economy is good, monetary policy is expansive, interest rates are trending down, and investors are diversifying their investment thesis by going into alternative asset classes, including commodities, bitcoin, and international as well as emerging markets. Additionally, we are witnessing significant geopolitical risk around the world, and that is helping to push gold and silver higher. These factors will continue to be strong catalysts for the metals market moving higher.

What is a surprise to us, however, is the fact that these metals are moving up (and their stocks) in the face of higher-trending interest rates (10-year and longer) these past few weeks and a US dollar that has been on a move higher as money pours into our country from the global markets. Typically, a strong US dollar would have a dampening effect on the metals markets. Given the recent strength of the US dollar and a rally in the metals, it only demonstrates how powerful the move in gold and silver lately has been.

Here is a chart of the recent strength of the US dollar:

What is in store for the rest of the year?

We are not in the prediction business (as many others claim to be). However, we remain aware that we are in an election year and are about to enter the best period to be invested in stocks seasonally. That makes us more optimistic about staying invested in the stock market and also in the metals, mining, and other alternative asset classes that are participating in this current bull market trend. See charts below describing the positive bias that continues:

Also, the internal technical picture of the market, as shown (above and below), is extremely positive for the stock market, at least for the next two months if not further out. See chart below.

New highs. On Monday, “the net percentage of S&P 500 members hitting 52-week highs reached the highest level (22%) since March... Forward returns for the S&P 500 have consistently been positive after strong readings in net new highs.”

We hope that this week’s overview provides some additional information and inputs to help you in your investment activity as we enter the last two months of the year. We plan to introduce some new areas for this column, including Fun Facts, in the near future. Given the length of this article, we did not want to take up any more of your time in this week’s column.

Everyone at MarketGauge wishes you a good week ahead. Hopefully, you enjoy a week of trading success while enjoying all the treasures and gifts that life provides us every day. Thanks for reading!

Risk-On

  • Markets were all generally up slightly on the week with the Russell outperforming, closing up over +2% on the week. The Dow and S&P closed on new all-time highs. (+)
  • Volume patterns remain fairly mixed, with the exception of the Russell, which looks poised to explode higher on price out of a multi-month compression range. (+)
  • 13 of the 17 sectors we track were up on the week, led by gold miners, homebuilders, and utilities. Surprisingly, semiconductors was the weakest sector, down -2% for the week. Only 2 of the 17 sectors are not in “bullish” phases. We are giving this an overall positive read for the market. (+)
  • Metals exploding higher was the theme for the week, with gold hitting new all-time highs. Energy was down sharply on the week, generally a good thing for the consumer. (+)
  • Market internals improved, with the McClellan Oscillator regaining positive territory for both the Nasdaq and S&P and the cumulative advance/decline making new highs for the S&P. (+)
  • The New High New Low ratio for the S&P improved significantly (and even more so for the Nasdaq) on a short-term basis and looking a bit more positive on the intermediate. (+)
  • The number of stocks above key moving averages improved and remains bullish, although on a short-term basis small caps are overbought. (+)
  • Both Value and Growth closed at new highs and in bullish phases. (+)
  • All the Modern Family members are in bullish phases. (+)
  • S&P color charts remain in gear, though are showing some slowing on a shorter-term basis. (+)

Neutral

  • Risk gauges moved back to neutral due to the strength in gold and utilities. (=)
  • Foreign equities were marginally down on the week with emerging markets still leading more established markets, likely bolstered by strength in metals and mining. (=)
  • Soft commodities and copper were down on the week, although remain in bull phases. (=)
  • Rates remain relatively flat on the week, although the yield curve is no longer inverted. (=)
  • The 1 to 3 month volatility reading flipped to a risk-on positive reading but cash volatility still remains in a bull phase, which is a cautionary read overall for sentiment indicators. (=)

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Icade 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Icade in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: Dynatrace: Observing Stronger Growth Ahead
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Dynatrace (NYSE:DT) is about to report its fiscal Q2 2025 earnings report on 7 November, premarket.

I believe that DT is well-positioned for a comeback. Not only are its revenue growth rates likely to reaccelerate in the second half of fiscal 2025, but also, its valuation today is no longer excessive.

In sum, I make the case that paying 38x forward free cash flow for DT makes sense, especially since it operates with no debt on its balance sheet.

In short, in the next several months, investors will look back to $55 per share as a low price point to enter this name.

Rapid Recap

Back in June, I said,

[...] the company's core business is highly profitable. What's more, the business holds no debt, and more than 5% of its market cap is made up of cash.

Buying DT at 27x this year's non-GAAP operating profits provides investors with a compelling risk-reward.

DT hasn't been an easy hold in 2024. It's easy now to should out the ''buy the dip'' moments, but doing that is neither helpful now, nor lucrative. What we must do now is focus on the future.

Dynatrace's Near-Term Prospects

Dynatrace is an observability platform for organizations to monitor the performance and security of their applications.

Its platform offers insights by leveraging data-driven automation to detect and resolve issues across cloud environments.

Dynatrace’s value proposition lies in its ability to deliver an actionable insights, enabling businesses to optimize user experiences and improve operational efficiency.

In the near term, Dynatrace is well-positioned for growth, driven by the increasing demand for observability solutions amid widespread cloud migration and AI adoption.

Even though the market is seemingly obsessed with only chasing the first layer of AI beneficiaries (namely the tier-1 chip player, Nvidia (NVDA)), I argue that the need for AI-powered platforms that reduce complexity creates a positive backdrop for Dynatrace to expand into, particularly as organizations seek resilience in software systems.

All that being said, keep in mind, that DT also faces significant near-term headwinds from rivals like Datadog (DDOG). Datadog, a key player in observability, that is also expanding its platform and focusing heavily on AI-driven insights similar to Dynatrace.

This creates pressure on Dynatrace to maintain its edge and actionable analytics, at a price that is competitive, especially with Datadog being known for its ease of use and appeal to developers.

With that background in mind, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Should Reaccelerate

Unless something goes substantially wrong in two weeks when Dynatrace reports its fiscal Q2 2025, I believe that Dynatrace is in a good position to upwards revise its fiscal H2 2025 revenue growth rates. This takes us to a couple of considerations.

Firstly, the obvious aspect. The comparables with fiscal H1 2024 are much easier than they were for fiscal H1 2024. That's something to keep in mind. In fact, fiscal Q4 2025 is up against the easiest comparable of this fiscal year. And then, next year, the comparables are even more attractive.

Secondly, and this one is more difficult to substantiate since we are still very early in the earnings season, and very few companies have yet reported their earnings, but I see some rooms in the sector that there appears to have already been a fair amount of software digestion in 2024, that should bode very nicely for 2025.

Either of those aspects alone and I would be more inclined to dismiss this bull thesis here. But both factors taken together buoy my optimism for DT. Particularly, when taken in light of its valuation, which we discuss next.

DT Stock Valuation -- 38x Forward Free Cash Flow

As an Inflection investor, we should always start our analysis thinking about the company's balance sheet. On this front, Dynatrace scores highly with more than $1 billion of cash and cash equivalents. What's more, the business holds no debt.

Not only does this mean that more than 5% of its market cap is made up of cash, but it also provides Dynatrace with options to make a large needle-moving bolt-on acquisition.

Moving on, Dynatrace's guidance implies that approximately $400 million to perhaps as much as $425 million could be on the cards this fiscal year.

This means that Dynatrace is priced at approximately 38x forward free cash flow. This is not a stretched valuation for a business that is able to deliver the sort of compelling growth rates that Dynatrace is delivering.

What's more, the graphic that follows to a large extent emphasizes my contention that Dynatrace isn't exuberantly priced.

Dynatrace doesn't compete against JFrog (FROG) but it does compete against Datadog (DDOG) and Elastic (ESTC). Furthermore, we shouldn't take this graphic in the literal sense, but rather another element on which to base our conclusion. What's the sort of multiple investors are willing to pay for exposure to SaaS / developer stocks?

As you can see above, Dynatrace is offered at a fair valuation for fresh capital seeking exposure to this business. That's a good position to be in, in my opinion.

The Bottom Line

As I look ahead, I believe that Dynatrace is set for a comeback.

The company's competitive edge in observability, along with its growing emphasis on AI-driven automation, positions it well for the increasing demand for resilient cloud and software systems.

While it has plenty of competitors, including Datadog, which presents a meaningful challenge, I'm confident that Dynatrace’s financial stability and ability to innovate will allow it to maintain its momentum.

With its solid cash position and no debt, Dynatrace can seize opportunities to drive further growth. After all, staying ahead of the curve in observability means keeping a close watch on what's next (get it? Dad joke!).

This is the punchline, I believe that 38x this year's free cash flow for DT is an attractive risk reward.

data.title: Tesla: Shift From The Land Of Oz To Reality
data.description:
My thesis

This is my investment thesis update for Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA). The first one was bearish, a Strong Sell. TSLA is currently around 5% cheaper than it was since my first call was published.

The company will release its Q3 2024 earnings soon, on October 23. With today's thesis update, I want to warn readers that there are a few indications that the earnings release might be disappointing. Based on the recent Q3 delivery numbers and highly likely no significant changes in the average selling prices, I think that Tesla is likely to miss revenue growth expectations. Since Tesla gained a massive market capitalization that is multiple times higher compared to the largest legacy automotive makers due to its unmatched revenue growth, I think that missing Q3 revenue estimates can be a significant bearish catalyst. The recent robotaxi event was met with unusual market pessimism, a rare occurrence for Tesla's major announcements. This shift in investor sentiment might indicate that Tesla has reached a stage where tangible products and concrete profits are becoming more crucial to investors than futuristic concepts and promises.

Finally, the valuation is still very high, looking both at the discounted cash flow, or DCF, and multiples. TSLA remains a Strong Sell, in my opinion.

TSLA stock analysis

We have the company's upcoming earnings call scheduled for October 23, which is quite soon. Therefore, today's thesis will be focused on my expectations around TSLA's Q3 earnings. According to quarterly consensus estimates, Wall Street expects the company to deliver a 10% YoY revenue growth, but the non-GAAP EPS to decline by around 9%.

The good part is that revenue growth is expected to continue accelerating after three previous quite challenging quarters. On the other hand, the EPS is expected to shrink, which is never good for shareholders' wealth. Moreover, Tesla has underperformed against consensus EPS estimates over the last four quarters with quite wide misses.

Since automotive sales account for approximately 81% of the company's total revenue, examining the number of EV units delivered in Q3 will likely provide strong evidence of TSLA's revenue dynamics. According to the official release, Tesla delivered 462,890 units in Q3 2024. This is around 6.4% higher on a YoY basis as the company delivered 435,059 units during the same quarter last year.

A 6.4% increase in physical volumes delivered is notably slower than the projected 10% revenue growth. We do not have details about the average selling price (ASP) in Q3, but decreased ASP was the number one headwind for TSLA's Q2 revenue, according to the presentation.

There are two reasons why it is quite unlikely that the situation with the ASP improved during Q3 2024. Let me remind you that there were two primary reasons why Tesla started offering aggressive discounts: high interest rates and intensifying competition in the EV space.

Both of these two unfavorable factors are still there. The Fed introduced its interest rate cut on September 18, meaning that interest rates were still at their peak during almost the entire quarter. The competition also becomes more intense as new EV models are entering the market. According to insideevs.com, Tesla Model Y (the company's by far most popular model) deliveries in the USA decreased by 9.1% YoY. This is a clear indication that competition is intensifying rapidly.

Therefore, with the restrictive monetary policy and intensifying competition, it is highly unlikely that the ASP YoY dynamic will be strong. Therefore, with a notable gap between the expected Q3 YoY revenue growth and actual growth in deliveries, there is a very high risk of TSLA delivering a negative revenue surprise. Due to Tesla's historically aggressive R&D spending and an inherently high proportion of fixed costs for an automotive company, it is highly likely that a potential revenue miss will also lead to an EPS miss.

Insiders bought zero TSLA shares over the last twelve months, which says a lot. I consider this information as a red flag because the stock saw a big drawdown in 2024. The stock dropped closer to $140 in April, which was a 42% drawdown compared to the stock price at the beginning of 2024. Even despite such a dip drawdown, none of the insiders bought the stock, which means that those with the most insight into the company's operations and potential challenges are still not optimistic about TSLA's short-term prospects.

Finally, TSLA bulls might argue that there have been quarters when, despite missing revenue or EPS estimates, the stock rallied after the earnings call. This, they might say, was due to CEO Elon Musk's ability to persuade investors that there are still numerous ambitious plans to conquer new blue oceans. This could have been the case, but the robotaxi event on October 10 suggests that the paradigm has shifted. Despite the impressive presentation with traditionally beautiful and futuristic prototypes, investors were likely not impressed. The fact that the stock declined by more than 7% since October 10, while the S&P 500 held up quite well, suggests that TSLA is currently at a stage in its business life cycle where investors prefer tangible results over futuristic promises. Therefore, I do not expect Elon Musk to charm investors during the earnings call, which might overshadow missing earnings estimates.

Intrinsic value calculation

Tesla became by far the world's largest automaker by market capitalization thanks to its aggressive revenue growth, especially during the pandemic. Therefore, the discounted cash flow (DCF) approach is the best fit to value this stock. Tesla's total $12 billion debt is insignificant compared to the above $700 billion market cap, making the cost of equity the most suitable to be used as a discount rate. The discount rate is calculated below using the CAPM formula.

Based on the above working, the discount rate is 14.68%. The base year $100.07 billion revenue projection is based on consensus estimates. According to Precedence Research, the global EV market is expected to observe a 23.4% long-term CAGR. This growth rate is incorporated for years 2-5, an aggressive assumption. Tesla's past five years' average FCF margin is 6.05%. I use this level flat because, with the intensifying competition, it will be extremely difficult for Tesla to drive a free cash flow margin. Since EV is a thriving industry, I give Tesla an optimistic 5% perpetual growth rate.

The first scenario suggests that Tesla's intrinsic value (IV) per share is around $61, significantly lower compared to the last close. The gap is large, and I might be too pessimistic.

Therefore, let me simulate another scenario with more aggressive assumptions. I think that a 23.4% revenue CAGR for the next five years is already very aggressive, so I prefer not to change it. For the second scenario, I am using a softer 12% discount rate and a much more aggressive 7% perpetual growth rate. I also incorporate a 100 bps FCF margin expansion annually.

Even with all these aggressive assumptions, TSLA is still around 25% overvalued. To justify its current valuation, an 8.5% perpetual growth rate must be incorporated. I find this growth rate unrealistic because of the intensifying competition in the EV industry.

What can go wrong with my thesis?

Some examples we're always able to hear about from optimists: are full-self driving, the energy segment, or the humanoid robot Optimus. Though I have no idea where these initiatives are going to go, there's no doubt that Elon Musk is a great CEO, and he has founded several innovative companies. Maybe I'm not a genius like Musk, and perhaps I'm missing something in my slow-motion judgment. If the trillion-dollar humanoid robot market grows to take off, Tesla might reap the rewards as a leading innovator. So, there is a lot of doubt regarding these new initiatives, but that doesn't have to translate only into negatives.

The last three years showed us that Tesla's revenue growth is significantly dependent on the monetary policy. Just look at the above chart - the company's revenue growth started deteriorating rapidly when the Fed started increasing interest rates. As I mentioned earlier, the Fed recently started cutting interest rates, which is positive for TSLA. While interest rates are still very high compared to levels we saw during the pandemic, the Fed's pivot occurred. Therefore, any information that the Fed is moving to a less restrictive monetary policy faster than expected can lead to substantial rallies for TSLA.

Summary

Tesla remains a Strong Sell before Q3 earnings due to a highly likely revenue miss against consensus forecasts and massive overvaluation.

data.title: Dynex Capital, Inc. 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Dynex Capital, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: CI&T: Well Positioned To Capture Digital Transformation Demand
data.description:
Summary

I am positive about CI&T (NYSE:CINT). My summarized thesis is that the business is in a very good position to take advantage of the business digital transformation trend (focus on AI initiatives) with its new Flow product and revamped sales structure. My belief is that CINT can see growth accelerate to mid-teens and 20% over the next two years.

Company overview

CINT provides digital transformation solutions and services for businesses. Such services and solutions include strategy, design, and software engineering services. The business has operations in two key regions: North American and Europe, which represent 53% of trailing 12 months ("TTM") revenue, and LATAM, which represents 42% of TTM revenue. The remaining is from Asia (4% of TTM revenue). CINT is not a new company to this industry, and throughout its operating history, it has worked with blue-chip clients such as Google, Ford, Nestle, and Johnson & Johnson.

CINT is well positioned to capture AI transformation demand

CINT may be at the very early stages of strong positive growth inflection over the coming decades. The main growth driver is the surging demand for AI applications, i.e., enterprises are rushing to equip themselves with AI capabilities to enhance their business. However, the problem is a lot of businesses and CIOs do not know how to leverage AI, and I think this knowledge gap is going to be a massive opportunity for CINT to capture.

The key enabling product is "Flow," an AI-powered platform that CINT launched recently (it is easier to understand what it is by watching this two-minute clip). Basically, Flow complements existing coding assistants to automate work within specific client demands. In other words, it gives clients the ability to know how to leverage AI capabilities with ease. This product has gained solid traction so far, and the various operating metrics shown above illustrate how effective the product is. Of which, I specifically highlight the fact that Flow delivers tangible improvements in productivity (up to 50% in lead time reduction, 3x quality improvements, and 2x productivity increase). Also note that this product was developed in close engagement with CINT’s top clients, which lends credence to the product quality in terms of client’s usability and data governance complexity.

The bigger implication is that Flow well equips CINT to: (1) gain wallet share as increased productivity often results in expanding engagements with existing clients; and 2) win new business deals as Flow reduces CINT overall implementation time (75% of CINT developers are using Flow). Ultimately, these enable CINT to ride on the vendor consolidation trend, in which I expect clients to consolidate more use cases for CINT’s product at the expense of less productive local vendors (as the improved productivity from using Flow has enabled clients to address more complex tasks).

Mentioned in the 2Q24 earnings call: And also play in favor of CI&T is a lot of vendor consolidation process. So I think during the previous years companies, especially large companies, increased the number of vendors and leading to a lot of complexities and overhead.

While CINT’s size relative to other digital transformation peers such as EPAM systems, Globant, Thoughtworks, Endava PLC, and Netcompany Group is much smaller (more than 2x smaller in terms of revenue), I believe this has worked in favor for CINT. Being smaller than competitors has enabled CINT to reposition the entire business to focus on leveraging this AI opportunity. This is to say, CINT can quickly allocate the majority of its resources to focus on this high-growth opportunity without being burdened with the need to address a long list of project backdrops and pipelines.

CINT has also massively restructured its sales strategy, which I am extremely positive about. If you look at CINT’s history, this business had no dedicated sales teams. New business deals were handled by employees that are responsible for both sales and delivery (i.e.., implementation). However, this year, the business adopted a new structure, shifting sales responsibilities to new regionalized sales teams along with key account managers. While this approach has increased sales-and-marketing costs, I believe the positive significantly outweighs this cost increase as management can better align incentives to drive up sales (i.e.., this team just needs to focus on hitting sales targets vs. the previous setting that needs to focus on sales and implementation). It also makes it easier for CINT to roll out new products as the sales team is incentivized to pitch new products to prospects vs. the previous setting, where employees may be less willing to do so (since it is more work on the implementation side of things).

Near-term growth outlook should get better

The concern I have about CINT is that growth has slowed sharply over in 4Q23 and 1Q24, down 15% in 4Q23 and 14% in 1Q24. The good news is that this weak growth sentiment seems to have gotten better. Firstly, y/y growth has recovered sharply back to -1% in 2Q24, and management comments about the demand outlook are very encouraging. Management noted that local clients are showing more stability on budgets and signs of a stabilizing demand trend that supports growth acceleration in 2025. CINT is also benefiting from the ramp-up of contracts with large clients, which further supports growth acceleration.

Mentioned in the 2Q24 earnings call: This strong growth was primarily driven by the expansion of our existing engagement with our top clients with additional contribution from ramp-ups of some new clients we recently acquired.

Valuation

I believe CINT is worth 17% more than the current share price. My target price is based on FY26 $291 million net income and a forward PE multiple of 22x.

Earnings bridge: Growth should accelerate to 15% in FY25 and 20% in FY26, supported by the growing demand for AI, CINT Flow product, and the revamped sales strategy. My growth estimates are based on management strong guidance for 12% y/y growth in 3Q24, which sets a baseline for potential mid-teens growth in FY25 and 20% growth in FY26.

Mentioned in the 2Q24 earnings call: Now, let me add some color to our business outlook for the next quarter and year. We expect our net revenue in the third quarter of 2024, to be at least BRL591 million on a reported basis, equivalent to an 11.7% growth in revenue compared to the third quarter of 2023, and a 4.5% increase on a sequential basis.

Valuation justification: While CINT is able to reallocate resources faster than peers, I agree its smaller scale should prevent it from trading at a premium to peers. Peers (as mentioned above) trade at an average of 22x forward PE with growth expectations of high single-digits to low teens. If CINT can demonstrate growth of mid-teens to 20% as I modeled, it should have no issues trading in line with peers, I believe.

Investment Risk

While CINT now has a more diversified client mix, the company’s top 10 clients still account for more than 40% of revenue. Accordingly, the loss of major clients or any reductions in revenues or contract scopes with them could have significant impacts for CINT. Worse-than-expected sales performance under CINT’s newly-revamped sales strategy would tend to negatively impact revenues and stock performance.

Conclusion

My positive view on CINT is because of its AI-powered platform, Flow, which, coupled with its revamped sales strategy, positions it well to capitalize on the increasing demand for digital transformation solutions. With a favorable growth outlook and a reasonable valuation, I think the upside is quite attractive over the next few years.

data.title: DSV A/S: Making Real Money Is The Core Competency Of DSVDF
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from this fund letter.

DSV A/S (OTCPK:DSDVF)

During the third quarter, we initiated a new position in the Danish freight forwarder DSV. DSV has come a long way from its origins as a Nordic road-hauler to become the world’s third largest freight forwarder, with a formidable track record of consolidating the fragmented global freight forwarding industry. We believe the company has an impressive culture that is systems-driven and returns-focused. DSV has generated an approximately 20% EPS CAGR over the past 10 years and is widely recognized as the best-in-class operator, with industry-leading growth and profit margins.

DSV emerged as the leading bidder in the auction of DB Schenker, a subsidiary of German state-owned Deutsche Bahn AG, and one of its largest competitors. DB Schenker is similar in size to DSV but only half as profitable. We believe the integration and synergy capture expected from this combination will follow a proven playbook and drive earnings accretion in excess of 30%. We have analyzed DSV’s many acquisitions and observed that they follow a pattern of swiftly migrating the target onto DSV’s IT system, culling low-margin business, and rightsizing the cost structure, resulting in the target’s margins reaching DSV’s best-in- class margins within two years.

The DB Schenker acquisition is taking place at an interesting time. Following a period of post-COVID earnings normalization and a CEO change, DSV’s stock was trading at an approximate 20% discount to both its lower-growth peers and its historical multiple. Following the deal, DSV will be the largest player in an industry in which scale brings tangible cost and network benefits. An example of this privileged competitive positioning is that DSV was selected as the exclusive logistics provider for Saudi Arabia’s NEOM project. We believe the joint venture between DSV and Saudi Arabia will provide end-to-end supply chain management, develop transport and logistic assets, and grow the company’s earnings power by about 15% by 2028.

We have spent time with Jens Lund, DSV’s long time COO/CFO who became CEO earlier this year and have found him to be laser-focused on creating shareholder value. Mr. Lund made a compelling case that rising complexity in global supply chains will benefit DSV, as it monetizes its unique network that guarantees capacity and on-time deliveries. In the freight forwarding industry, simple load, A-to-B transportation is barely profitable. The real money is made from value-added services such as customs clearance, load consolidation and intervention when problems occur, a core competency of DSV. We believe DSV can earn more than 100 DKK per share in 2027 and see significant upside for one of Europe’s best companies.

The information contained herein is being provided to the investors in Third Point Investors Limited (the “Company”), a feeder fund listed on the London Stock Exchange that invests substantially all of its assets in Third Point Offshore Fund, Ltd (“Third Point Offshore”). Third Point Offshore is managed by Third Point LLC (“Third Point” or “Investment Manager”), an SEC-registered investment adviser headquartered in New York. Third Point Offshore is a feeder fund to the Third Point Offshore Master Fund L.P. in a master-feeder structure. Third Point LLC , an SEC registered investment adviser, is the Investment Manager to the Funds.

Unless otherwise specified, all information contained herein relates to the Third Point Offshore Master Fund L.P. inclusive of legacy private investments. P&L and AUM information are presented at the feeder fund level where applicable. Sector and geographic categories are determined by Third Point in its sole discretion.

Performance results are presented net of management fees, brokerage commissions, administrative expenses, and accrued performance allocation, if any, and include the reinvestment of all dividends, interest, and capital gains. While performance allocations are accrued monthly, they are deducted from investor balances only annually or upon withdrawal. From the inception of Third Point Offshore through December 31, 2019, the fund's historical performance has been calculated using the actual management fees and performance allocations paid by the fund. The actual management fees and performance allocations paid by the fund reflect a blended rate of management fees and performance allocations based on the weighted average of amounts invested in different share classes subject to different management fee and/or performance allocation terms. Such management fee rates have ranged over time from 1% to 2% per annum. The amount of performance allocations applicable to any one investor in the fund will vary materially depending on numerous factors, including without limitation: the specific terms, the date of initial investment, the duration of investment, the date of withdrawal, and market conditions. As such, the net performance shown for Third Point Offshore from inception through December 31, 2019 is not an estimate of any specific investor’s actual performance. For the period beginning January 1, 2020, the fund’s historical performance shows indicative performance for a new issues eligible investor in the highest management fee (2% per annum) and performance allocation (20%) class of the fund, who has participated in all side pocket private investments (as applicable) from March 1, 2021 onward. The inception date for Third Point Offshore Fund Ltd is December 1, 1996. All performance results are estimates and past performance is not necessarily indicative of future results.

The net P&L figures are included because of the SEC’s new marketing rule and guidance. Third Point does not believe that this metric accurately reflects net P&L for the referenced sub-portfolio group of investments as explained more fully below. Specifically, net P&L returns reflect the allocation of the highest management fee (2% per annum), in addition to leverage factor multiple, if applicable, and incentive allocation rate (20%), and an assumed operating expense ratio (0.3%), to the aggregate underlying positions in the referenced sub-portfolio group’s gross P&L. The management fees and operating expenses are allocated for the period proportionately based on the average gross exposures of the aggregate underlying positions of the referenced sub-portfolio group. The implied incentive allocation is based on the deduction of the management fee and expense ratio from Third Point Offshore fund level gross P&L attribution for the period. The incentive allocation is accrued for each period to only those positions within the referenced sub-portfolio group with i) positive P&L and ii) if during the current MTD period there is an incentive allocation. In MTD periods where there is a reversal of previously accrued incentive allocation, the impact of the reversal will be based on the previous month’s YTD accrued incentive allocation. The assumed operating expense ratio noted herein is applied uniformly across all underlying positions in the referenced sub-portfolio group given the inherent difficulty in determining and allocating the expenses on a sub-portfolio group basis. If expenses were to be allocated on a sub- portfolio group basis, the net P&L would likely be different for each referenced investment or sub-portfolio group, as applicable.

While the performances of the fund has been compared here with the performance of well-known and widely recognized indices, the indices have not been selected to represent an appropriate benchmark for the fund whose holdings, performance and volatility may differ significantly from the securities that comprise the indices. Past performance is not necessarily indicative of future results. All information provided herein is for informational purposes only and should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. All investments involve risk including the loss of principal. This transmission is confidential and may not be redistributed without the express written consent of Third Point LLC and does not constitute an offer to sell or the solicitation of an offer to purchase any security or investment product. Any such offer or solicitation may only be made by means of delivery of an approved confidential offering memorandum.

Specific companies or securities shown in this presentation are meant to demonstrate Third Point’s investment style and the types of industries and instruments in which we invest and are not selected based on past performance. The analyses and conclusions of Third Point contained in this presentation include certain statements, assumptions, estimates and projections that reflect various assumptions by Third Point concerning anticipated results that are inherently subject to significant economic, competitive, and other uncertainties and contingencies and have been included solely for illustrative purposes. No representations express or implied, are made as to the accuracy or completeness of such statements, assumptions, estimates or projections or with respect to any other materials herein. Third Point may buy, sell, cover, or otherwise change the nature, form, or amount of its investments, including any investments identified in this letter, without further notice and in Third Point’s sole discretion and for any reason. Third Point hereby disclaims any duty to update any information in this letter.

This letter may include performance and other position information relating to once activist positions that are no longer active but for which there remain residual holdings managed in a non-engaged manner. Such holdings may continue to be categorized as activist during such holding period for portfolio management, risk management and investor reporting purposes, among other things.

Information provided herein, or otherwise provided with respect to a potential investment in the Funds, may constitute non-public information regarding Third Point Investors Limited, a feeder fund listed on the London Stock Exchange, and accordingly dealing or trading in the shares of the listed instrument on the basis of such information may violate securities laws in the United Kingdom, United States and elsewhere.

New Series (Excludes Legacy Private Investments)3

1 Through September 30, 2024. Please note there is a one-month lag in performance reflected for the CS HF Event-Driven Index

2 Annualized Return from inception (December 1996 for TP Offshore and quoted indices).

3“New Series (Excludes Legacy Private Investments)” uses the existing series track record form inception through May 31, 2023.

Returns from June 1, 2023 and onward exclude legacy private investments.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sangamo Follow-Up: Giro-Vec And STAC-BBB? Check. Iso-Vec... Trifecta Soon?
data.description:
On July 23, 2024, I wrote an article highlighting that Sangamo Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:SGMO) had 3 lead assets which could catapult the company’s turnaround, resulting in a potential billion-dollar valuation. 2 of 3 have taken place so far. At the time of the article, Sangamo was trading ~$0.40 a share, or ~$82M valuation, due to its low level of cash and history of underdelivering.

Pfizer Inc. (PFE) then reported positive phase 3 results for giro-vec, setting SGMO up to potentially earn up to $220M in milestones over the upcoming 12–18 months. Sangamo followed up this announcement with one for a STAC-BBB deal with Roche/Genentech (OTCQX:RHHBY): $50M up front and $1.9B in potential milestones.

The company has indicated that an iso-vec deal is in the works. I’ll share below why I think a deal for this third asset would be transformative and will take place soon. After Sangamo’s initial 200% pop, it has settled back down to ~$0.90 a share, or a $190M valuation.

Due to the high-risks and unpredictability of these types of biotechs, an investment in Sangamo Therapeutics, Inc. stock is not suitable for most investors.

Introduction

In my initial article on Sangamo Therapeutics for Seeking Alpha, I highlighted the 3 assets I deemed critical to SGMO’s success (and survival) and most likely to be addressed before the end of the year. They were giro-vec for Hemophilia A; isa-vec for Fabry Disease and STAC-BBB for CNS diseases: a potential trifecta that could steer the company out of bankruptcy risk and into a potential $1B+ valuation.

I strongly encourage anyone reading this article to go back and review the initial one for a deeper clinical dive into each asset and its respective market, as well as the rationale for why I selected them.

2 out of the 3 predictions have come to pass (giro-vec and STAC-BBB) and the limited, but positive updates we have been given on the third (isa-vec) indicate that the potential financial impact I was expecting should be as good or better than anticipated. Therefore, in this article, I will review SGMO’s progress on each of these 3 assets and why a near-term update on isa-vec should complete the trifecta. Then I will briefly touch on the likelihood of SGMO’s next 3 assets moving further into clinical trials with enough cash to proceed over the next couple of years.

Stock Moves

First, though, I want to reiterate my thoughts on the company’s stock moves which I shared in my original article’s “Comments” section since both announcements now have been made by the company.

The initial pop from the giro-vec update saw the stock more than double. Why not higher? Because while Pfizer, Inc’s update was positive, SGMO would not be receiving any funds for at least another 6–9 months. Therefore, the immediate cash needs were not met. A legitimate question out there was, will the company raise on this update and/or monetize the potential milestones/royalties?

The second pop from the STAC-BBB announcement took this stock higher to ~$1.15/share at one point. This latter achievement is different from the first for two important reasons: 1) The cash upfront averted short-term bankruptcy, and the partnering with Roche/Genentech meant a top-tier biotech had validated the STAC-BBB technology. So why the partial stock price give-back? It’s pretty straight-forward. Until SGMO sells/partners isa-vec for cash and/or receives the giro-vec milestones, the company will be viewed just as a going-concern financially. 2) Until the market knows whether SGMO is going to conduct a follow-on offering and/or a reverse stock split, it is safer and potentially required (due to the stock’s sub-$1 a share price) for institutional investors to wait on the sidelines. More details below.

In my initial article, I had decided to enter the stock at ~$0.40/s or ~$82M valuation before the announcements because the data suggested success. 2 out of 3 is nice, but the trifecta payout should be larger as well as provide a path to longer-term success. In my subsequent comments to investor’s questions and feedback, I have shared that I maintain an oversized position in my portfolio, which has now grown to ~9% because the path to a $1B+ valuation is clearer.

Volatility: Momentum investors are trading in and out of SGMO causing up to 5-10% stock moves in days because they can. Until a top biotech institutional anchor investor or larger biotech buys-in, this volatility will remain, in my opinion. I am fine with that. I am going to hold my entire position into the isa-vec announcement and based on the data/deal announced, I may trim depending on the peak stock move since I can see more deals/collaborations coming over the next 6–12 months.

Two other important notes to make: 1) BlackRock (BLK) announced on 07/08/24 that they had sold ~9.4M shares recently, taking their ownership down from ~6.8% to ~2.3%. Then Biogen (Nasdaq: BIIB) announced on 08/14/24 that they had sold 11.4M shares and took their position in SGMO down to 3.25M shares, or ~1.6% of the company. I am not concerned with either sale because BlackRock most likely had to do some selling when the stock price remained below $1 and valuation below $100M; and in my opinion, BIIB sold most of its shares likely because they lost out on the STAC-BBB/Tau asset to RHHBY.

To put that in perspective, that was ~20.8M shares sold by just two holders, which represented close to 10% of SGMO’s 207.5M outstanding shares as of 04/08/24. That is a lot of volume to clear, and it is most likely that both entities sold into every rally they could, blunting attempts for SGMO to keep more of its recent gains.

Something else I will be watching for in 4 weeks is, did any of my top 10 hedge fund investors pick up shares in SGMO by the end of 3Q’24 since we will know by November 15th when they post their Schedule 13s. (Any purchases of less than 5% of a company by an investor or another company since the start of 3Q’24, do not have to be reported until that 11/15/24 date.)

Pipeline Update on 3 Assets Discussed in Last Article: Trifecta?

Giroctocogene fitelparvovec (giro-vec)/PF-07055480/SB-525:

(Please refer to my previous article for more in-depth clinical information on the asset and disease state.)

Giro-vec is a GTx designed to utilize a recombinant AAV6 vector to deliver a functional F8 gene to a patient’s liver following a single treatment. PFE acquired the worldwide rights to giro-vec on 05/10/17 for $70M up front and up to $300M for development and commercialization milestones.

Enough efficacy and safety data were released in the 07/24/24 update to justify PFE submitting giro-vec for approval. And if and when they do, PFE’s filings should bring SGMO up to $220M in milestones over the next year or so. As mentioned above, this de-risking of giro-vec was the cause for SGMO’s initial spike in stock price. Some skeptics will have you believe that PFE might not file giro-vec. Here are some of their excuses and why I think they are mistaken.

“Biomarin Pharmaceutical, Inc’s (Nasdaq: BMRN) commercialization of rival Roctavian has been a failure to date.” True, but ignoring why it failed and trying to equate the 2 companies’ ability to launch drugs is extremely short-sighted. 1) Roctavian at a minimum has durability questions, and it has been suggested that HCPs are currently warehousing patients until they see giro-vec’s full data set. 2) PFE remains one of the best sales and marketing machines in the business. They have an existing Hemophilia franchise, and giro-vec fits nicely into and complements an existing sales force. Further, if PFE prices giro-vec around or under Roctavian as well as makes it more available to the key institutions treating the disease, BMRN is screwed.

“Giro-vec’s topline efficacy and safety data released to date are positive, but we didn’t get to see everything.” No kidding! There is a reason it is called a “topline” release. A company will spend months sifting through the data after these releases to determine additional advantages and/or weaknesses the data might reveal. Serious biotech investors know that biopharma companies hold important data back to be presented at a future major conference and/or for future investment opportunities (and the American Society of Hematology [ASH] Annual Meeting will be in December). Had there been a major negative Adverse Event [AE] or trend across the study, PFE would have been obligated to share it. This doesn’t mean something negative might be identified going forward, but if the “base case” to date is that the data is good enough to file, then there is also the potential for the full update to be even better regarding durability and safety.

“PFE’s rare disease efforts have been disappointing to date.” Agreed! But most of that has been from internal projects in the clinic and not in commercialization, such as forda-vec's recent failure in DMD, and subsequent termination of that program. However, these clinical failures are completely irrelevant to a giro-vec potential filing. In fact, my opinion is that these clinical failures put more of a spotlight on and/or pressure for giro-vec and the hemophilia franchise to be a commercial success for PFE, including the recent announcement on 09/25/24 that PFE was voluntarily pulling Oxbryta/voxelator from worldwide markets. Oxbryta was the Sickle Cell drug that PFE acquired in its purchase of Global Blood Therapeutics (formerly Nasdaq: GBT) in August 2022 for $5.4B. PFE needs a successful sales launch and growth from its hemophilia franchise. SGMO just needs PFE to file.

STAC-BBB capsid:

(Please refer to last article for more in-depth clinical information on the asset and disease state.)

STAC-BBB is a unique capsid that was identified using SGMO’s SIFTER platform. As a reminder, at the American Society for Gene & Cell Therapy [ASGCT] on 05/08/24, SGMO showed that STAC-BBB exhibits 700-fold higher neuronal mRNA expression relative to AAV9, enabling it to deliver a corrected gene across the blood-brain-barrier in a non-human primate [NHP]. No other company has #s anywhere close to this. And about a week later, the CEO shared that 10 parties were interested in deal discussions. In my last article, I highlighted in the “Order of Operations” section that I hoped SGMO would announce a collaboration for a STAC-BBB asset after the giro-vec update because:

“the sooner SGMO can get a deal done for just one of the potential CNS diseases that STAC-BBB can deliver the correct gene for, the sooner its long-term cash needs are answered and the company can focus on execution. I could see the first deal being announced either before the end of the year or by JPM in early January.”

~2 weeks later and on the same day as the company’s 2Q’24 earnings announcement, SGMO announced a STAC-BBB deal with Genentech on 08/06/24 for $50M upfront and up to $1.9B in development and commercial milestones plus potential royalties. While the $50M upfront is less than what some investors wanted and less than the prior BIIB that brought in a $125M licensing fee upfront and $225M equity investment for two neuro assets, this deal is critical to the rebirth of SGMO far beyond its monetary value: 1) it validates the STAC-BBB platform, and 2) it reestablishes some credibility to the CEO and board after an abysmal couple of years.

Kudos to RHHBY for more of a wait and see deal loaded on the backend. But keep in mind, SGMO was in a pretty rough short-term cash position. They took the best partner/deal they could get at the time, and RHHBY is a BIG name. I am pretty confident SGMO won’t announce another STAC-BBB deal until after either an isa-vec deal has been announced and/or PFE announces its filing of giro-vec. At that point, SGMO will have at least 1 and possibly 2 years of financing secured and more leverage to negotiate. Meanwhile, I anticipate additional clinical updates on STAC-BBB and the targets SGMO will apply it to.

Isaralgagene civaparvovec (isa-vec)/ST-290 for Fabry Disease:

(Please refer to last article for more in-depth clinical information.)

Isa-vec is a GTx designed to carry the DNA for human alpha-Gal A to the liver of Fabry patients by a single IV infusion so that the liver can start producing functional alpha-Gal-A. At the time of my initial article, we really only had the ph1/2 STAAR clinical trial (NCT04046224) data on 24 dosed patients who were either on ERT, been off it for 6 months or more, or were ERT-naïve based on the study’s primary and secondary outcomes and the FDA’s commitment that:

“Following a Type D meeting with the FDA, SGMO announced on 02/12/24 that only a single pivotal trial of 25 patients would be required to prove isa-vec’s success, and that there would be no head-to-head with an existing ERT or control arm (SOC or placebo).”

Back in July, I put a monetary value on a potential isa-vec deal at a couple of hundred million dollars based on the above. What SGMO has intimated since then could be truly game changing to all Fabry patients, regardless of current or future treatment. Specifically, on the same call the company announced its 2Q’24 earnings and STAC-BBB deal, it also shared that:

“Sangamo continues to amass encouraging clinical data, including evidence of improvements in kidney function. In the 18 patients treated for more than one year, a statistically significant rise in both mean and median eGFR levels was observed in male and female treated patients, based on preliminary findings. Anticipate sharing updated clinical data in the coming months.”

If isa-vec can truly reverse the kidney damage and/or improve patient outcomes in Fabry disease, this asset could become a billion-dollar blockbuster on its own. As I shared in the Comments section of my last article, there was the 14th Annual Fabry Family Education Conference this past week that might have been a nice place to present a Fabry update. However, the American Society of Nephrology Meeting [ASN] known as Kidney Week starts in a couple of days on 10/23/24. Talk about a perfect time to provide an isa-vec kidney update!

The Next 3 Pipeline Assets with Potential Value

SGMO was forced to place several assets on hold or to cancel due to its awful finances. The first two programs mentioned below have been moved forward despite the company’s financial situation due to the demonstration of early, but positive clinical results. With additional cash, they should continue their march into the clinic. The third asset was paused because it drained a ton of cash and no longer aligned with the company’s clinical focus. And while it is possible that the MINT platform could be one of the most valuable assets SGMO now has long-term, I am going to ascribe no value to it for now because it is so early in development.

Nav1.7 target for chronic neuropathic pain: Utilizes SGMO’s proprietary Zinc Finger repressors to target SCN9A and a STAC-BBB capsid to deliver it. The goal is to target and reduce pain signals from reaching the brain. While Vertex Pharmaceuticals (Nasdaq: VRTX) has an asset nearing potential approval for acute pain that targets a similar sodium channel (Nav1.8), that asset is oral and for moderate-to-severe acute pain. SGMO should file an IND by yearend for this asset, meaning it would be in the clinic and providing safety data potentially in 2025.

ST-504 for Prion Disease: This asset utilizes Zink Finger repressors to reduce the production of prion protein. Additional data was presented at ASGCT ’24 and a CTA is scheduled to be filed in 4Q ’25, so the asset can be in the clinic in ’26. While there are not many patients diagnosed with Prion disease, it is fatal and incurable.

Car-Treg: I mention this asset because the program no longer fits into SGMO’s plans, and much of the hard-assets (people and labs) have been let go or paused. SGMO needs to find the right cell-therapy partner for this asset to get fair-value. And when the company was short on cash, I am sure the vultures were swooping in. Once 2 years of runway are established, I can see a deal of some sort taking place. However, determining a value at this point would be difficult without a further clinical update by SGMO.

Discussion on Financials

In my opinion, cash and burn rate are the only two metrics that matter currently to my investment in SGMO. In the 2Q '24 Financial Results, the company shared they had cash of ~$27.8 in cash and a cash burn rate of ~$36.2M. It is easy to see how concerning this would be if reviewed in a vacuum.

At the time of my last article, SGMO appeared on the verge of insolvency. It had made significant cuts to headcount and reorganized the pipeline to focus on the lead assets mentioned above. The earlier assets had potential and were being “optimized” for future clinical work, but all eyes were waiting for the giro-vec update and the potential cash it could bring.

Since then, SGMO’s STAC-BBB deal with RHHBY provided $50M upfront, which should provide a cash runway to take the company through 1Q ’25. Should PFE submit giro-vec to the FDA early in 2025 followed by additional filings world-wide including the EU, these filings will trigger the up to $220M milestone payments to SGMO that will in turn provide SGMO with the capital it needs through at least 2025. Sorry naysayers, but short-term insolvency is off the table.

Should SGMO also complete the trifecta I spoke about in July, and isa-vec is now sold or partnered for a couple of hundred million dollars upfront, SGMO will have enough cash to run safely for at least 2 years (over $400M). They should also have a handful of very promising assets they can continue to move through the clinic and potentially receive billions of dollars in milestones set to be achieved over the coming years…all without the need to do another deal. And this does not include the MINT platform (another asset basically thrown in for free at this point).

Without debating the current or future value of each individual follow-on asset discussed above; and/or the future upfront amounts SGMO could get for additional STAC-BBB deals; or even the massive potential that the MINT platform could eventually unlock for the company in large-scale gene editing, I am comfortable reiterating my projection that SGMO is on the path to a $1B+ valuation over the next year or so over its current $192M.

Regarding a potential reverse-split, as I shared in my last letter:

“More shares coming! On 06/04/24, SGMO released the notes from its Annual Meeting. One of the topics to vote on was to increase the number of common shares to 960M from 640M which passed. Considering the company had ~203M shares after its Direct Offering, my gut says that 1) There is now interest (and certainly a need) for more shares from potential partners and/or investors; and 2) We may see a reverse-split in the near future. My reasoning? Even a 5x from here would take SGMO’s stock to ~400M valuation, but only a $2/s range without any further dilution. That sub-$5 stock price would still prevent large institutional investors from owning the company’s shares.

Therefore, I can see the company raising the funds it can from an isa-vec/Fabry deal over the next few weeks/couple months; factoring in the giro-vec milestones around the same time; and then conducting another follow-on/PIPE offering shortly thereafter with well-known biotech-focused funds who can purchase stock at any price. SGMO could then initiate a 1-for-5 or 1-for-10 reverse-split; leaving the shares in the $10-$20 range just in time for the company to announce partnership deals on the new BBB-penetrating capsid. I know it sounds like a lot, but for a company that has been through the ringer and now actually has a couple of commercially viable assets and an impressive CNS pipeline, the chance for success at SGMO has never been higher due to the de-risking of its two lead two assets. Thus, I believe there is a clear path to at least a $1B valuation over the next 12-24 months and maybe more.”

The fact that SGMO has not conducted a reverse-split yet might indicate they are trying to avoid it. But it is really difficult right now to say whether it is coming. Realistically, the company could raise enough cash here to get to the $5 per share mark including updated clinical asset valuations, which would in turn open the investment doors to a whole new (returning) investor group. We shall see.

Next Order of Operations?

If I was advising SGMO, here is how I would try to play out the next several months:

First, as I shared above, I can see the isa-vec update being announced over the next couple of days/ weeks. This should generate a positive move in the stock price.

Second, a deal for isa-vec could follow at or around the same time, but I would think certainly before JPM in January. Similar to what I shared in my initial article for a potential STAC-BBB deal, the actual terms of an isa-vec deal will likely determine the new valuation of SGMO. Personally, I would like to see more cash upfront on this one and fewer milestones. However, a steady stream of revenue would not be too bad either.

Third, I could see the biopharma collaborator for isa-vec acquiring a stake in SGMO at a value higher than at the pre-clinical update. Therefore, SGMO would be best served to share the data, allow the stock to rise, take a deal and then an investment at an even higher valuation.

Fourth, I would not be surprised to see PFE be the acquirer of isa-vec to get into that segment of rare disease. Sanofi (Nasdaq: SNY), a favorite serial acquirer of mine, is out because of their existing Fabry portfolio. However, as I shared before, the top 10 biopharmas out there have an interest in gene therapy and the kidney space [hi, Novartis (NYSE: NVS)].

Risks

The two greatest risks I see for SGMO to clear over the next 3–6 months are the giro-vec clinical update and subsequent FDA filing, and the isa-vec clinical update and subsequent potential deal for the asset.

If the additional giro-vec data is negative and/or PFE does not file the drug for approval, the loss of up to $220M in cash milestone payments would significantly impact SGMO’s cash position in 2025. I believe this possibility is highly unlikely. As stated above, the data shared to date by PFE is positive enough to file, and PFE is taking the steps to build a complete hemophilia franchise.

If the isa-vec update does not live up to its hype and/or a deal with unfavorable terms is announced, then SGMO would most likely have to raise additional cash in 2025 at a disadvantaged position. Again, based on the data publicly available to date, including FDA guidance and assistance to SGMO in a European filing, I think a negative update is unlikely.

Beyond that, investors need to keep in mind that SGMO is still a high-potential risk, high-potential reward biotech and that because of this, a blowup in the clinical is always possible. Therefore, an investment in SGMO is probably not suitable for most investors.

Conclusion

I continue to like Sangamo Therapeutics, Inc.’s prospects for success, and based on the available data in the public domain, I am comfortable with my current investment in the company. The next couple of days and months should enable the company to continue its successful turnaround, and I will certainly be watching attentively from the sidelines.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Publicis Groupe: Still Attractive Despite The Run-Up
data.description:
Introduction

As it has been over eighteen months since I last discussed Publicis Groupe (OTCQX:PUBGY) (OTCQX:PGPEF), I think an update is long overdue. In the summer of 2020, when the COVID pandemic was raging and the world economy came to a standstill, Publicis was trading below 30 EUR per share. Fortunately, the capex-light entity recouped a lot of its lost ground pretty fast, and the stock is now trading at in excess of 100 EUR per share. The total return since that 2020 article comes in at almost +270% including about 200% in capital gains, while it also remains a decent dividend payer.

This article is a follow-up to previous coverage. I'd suggest reading up on Publicis by going through my older articles.

As a reminder, one share of PUBGY represents 0.25 shares of Publicis. Meanwhile, a share of PGPEF represents one underlying share of Publicis. I will discuss the results of Publicis in Euro as that's the currency the company reports its financial results in, while the main listing of the company is on Euronext Paris. It is trading in Paris with PUB as its ticker symbol, and the average daily volume exceeds 350,000 shares for a monetary value of just over 35M EUR per day.

The company still is a cash flow giant

Publicis' financial results are a positive surprise. While the company only publishes detailed financial statements every six months, it also provides quarterly trading updates and in this article I will first focus on the half-year results before adding color based on the Q3 trading update.

In the first semester, Publicis reported a total revenue of approximately 7.65B EUR, which included roughly 962M EUR in pass-through revenue. And although the operating costs increased at a faster pace than the revenue, Publicis was still able to report a very respectable 1.4B EUR in operating income, and a 6% increase in its operating margin (after taking depreciation and amortization expenses into consideration).

Fortunately, the impairment losses remained limited this time, and that helped the operating income to jump by almost 20% to just over 1B EUR, while Publicis also generated a net finance income of 39M EUR. All these elements resulted in a pre-tax income of 1.04B EUR and a net profit of 780M EUR of which 773M EUR was attributable to the shareholders of Publicis. Based on the 250.7M shares outstanding, this resulted in an EPS of 3.08 EUR. An increase of just under 25% compared to the 2.48 EUR per share the company generated in H1 2023.

In my previous articles, I was predominantly focusing on Publicis' ability to generate a positive free cash flow, and that definitely still is the case. In the first half of the year, the company reported a negative operating cash flow of minus 556M EUR, but this included the traditional 1.63B EUR working capital build-up, while Publicis also paid 376M EUR in cash taxes although only 256M EUR was due, based on the H1 income statement. On the other hand, we should deduct the 29M EUR in net interest expenses (including the interest on lease liabilities), as well as the 182M EUR lease repayments. This means the adjusted operating cash flow in the first half of this year was approximately 982M EUR.

The cash flow statement shown above also indicates the total capex was approximately 120M EUR (excluding M&A) which means the underlying net free cash flow generated in the first semester was approximately 862M EUR. Adjusted for the stake attributable to the non-controlling interests, the net free cash flow attributable to the common shareholders of Publicis was approximately 978M EUR, or 3.90 EUR per share.

The Q3 trading update was positive

The company also published a trading update last week, and Publicis was able to upgrade its full-year guidance despite encountering some challenging macro headwinds.

The company now expects an organic growth rate of 5.5% this year, which is a rather important improvement compared to the previously expected 5% organic growth. Additionally, Publicis continues to expect 1.8-1.9B EUR in net free cash flow this year, which appears to be realistic, given its H1 performance.

Investment thesis

While I understand the reasoning behind the decision of other authors to move to a 'hold' on Publicis, I think the very strong free cash flow result still warrants a 'buy'. A very 'light' buy, but I think Publicis is still attractive on pullbacks because even at the current share price of just over 100 EUR, the stock is still trading at a free cash flow yield of around 7.75-8% which, in the current climate of decreasing risk-free interest rates, is still pretty appealing.

Of course, there are also risks here; if the world economy continues to slow down, PR budgets will for sure be cut, but I like how Publicis has navigated through the more difficult times before. With a cash position of 3.08B EUR and a net cash position (excluding lease liabilities) of approximately 50M EUR the balance sheet was very robust at the end of the first semester, but investors are obviously warned working capital changes and Publicis' M&A hunger will have an impact on its financial position.

I currently have no position in Publicis anymore, as I monetized my long position a while ago. But seeing how strong the company is performing, I have added it back to my shortlist, hoping for a good entry point.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 3 Reasons Imperial Brands Can Keep Rising
data.description:
Imperial Brands (OTCQX:IMBBY) (OTCQX:IMBBF) has been a standout tobacco stock over the past year. With gains of over 42%, its performance is now head and shoulders above that of its peers. Among the big five tobacco stocks by market capitalisation [MCap] (see chart below), Philip Morris (PM) comes a distant second, with its price gains 13.3 percentage points less than IMBBY.

Even when I last wrote about it in June, the stock was doing very well. But its price returns were still behind Japan Tobacco (OTCPK:JAPAY). With its ~20% rise since, however, it has clearly more than caught up. Despite this, though, the stock isn't done yet. Here are three reasons why it can continue to rise further.

#1. Positive full-year trading update

After robust results in the first half of its last financial year (H1 FY24), Imperial Brands continued to please investors with its recent trading update preceding the full year FY24 (year ending September 2024) results. The stock saw the second biggest gain year-to-date [YTD] of 4.7% on this release, smaller only than the one seen in May following its H1 FY24 results.

The company confirmed that its performance in FY24 is in line with guidance, which targets "further improvement in adjusted operating profit growth to support a mid-single-digit constant currency CAGR over FY23-FY25" (see table below).

The highlight of the update, however, is the progress with next generation products (NGPs). After seeing a 16.8% YoY rise in H1 FY24 on a constant currency basis, the company now says that "NGP net revenue is expected to grow in the range of 20-30% at constant currency".

This reflects acceleration in the segment's revenue growth in H2 2024 and is an important development considering that NGPs contributed to just 3.9% of the company's revenues in H1 FY24. To put it in context, Imperial Brands lags far behind peers like Philip Morris and British American Tobacco (BTI) in terms of the contribution of NGPs. PM is particularly noteworthy, with 40% of its revenues garnered from smoke-free products.

#2. Increased dividends and share buybacks

The company's dividend increase also goes in its favour. Along with the trading update, it released the schedule for dividend payouts in the next financial year (see table below), which shows a massive 27% YoY increase. With expected payments of GBP 1.89, expected payments for the next year bring the stock's forward dividend yield to 8.2%. This is higher than the 7.8% estimated the last time around.

Also, this forward yield is second only to British American Tobacco, which is at 8.6%. Further, it's higher than the average forward yield for the big five tobacco stocks at 7%. In line with its multi-year programme, the company also increased its share buyback for the current financial year by 7% year-on-year (YoY) to USD 1.25 billion. This is a non-trivial ~5% of its current market capitalisation that could contribute to some further stock price uptick.

#3. Attractive Market multiples

With its sustained price rise, IMBBY's forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is now at 9.54x, which is much higher than the 7.3x the last I checked. However, its relative ranking among tobacco stocks remains attractive. Much like in June, it's still trading lower than PM, JAPAY and Altria (MO).

My estimates put PM's forward P/E is at 18.8x and JAPAY at 15.9x, based on its profit forecast of JPY 475 billion (USD 3.17 billion), while analysts estimates on Seeking Alpha put MO at 9.72x. BTI continues to be the only one among the big five tobacco stocks that's trading much lower, at 7.48x. In other words, it's still attractively placed compared with peers.

Also, the stock's trailing twelve months [TTM] GAAP P/E ratio at 10.3x is lower than the decadal average of 10.5x. Even if we take into account the fact that the prospects for tobacco stocks have reduced in recent years due to structural changes in the industry, the fact remains that IMBBY is still trading at lower levels than even seen in 2023 (see chart below).

The market rally risk

However, in the short-term the stock can still plateau going by the expected uptick in stock markets in 2024. One reason for not just IMBBY's, but almost the entire tobacco set's solid performance YTD, has been market uncertainty on signs of macroeconomic weakness and a slow path towards monetary easing.

But the last quarter of the year has historically seen higher increases for the S&P 500 (SP500) compared to the rest of the year on higher consumer spending, as I had mentioned in my article on Philip Morris recently (linked in the section on Market Multiples). With the index already having made gains of 23% YTD, this has been a good year already. And by the looks of it, more positivity is possible. This can work against defensives like tobacco stocks.

That said, though, there's no fundamental reason for IMBBY's upward climb to stop. Unless of course, there are more challenges to the growth in its vaping market in the US on marketing denial, as has been seen in the past. That's not a fresh concern for now, however. In the meantime, IMBBY's unique selling point is its hefty dividend yield, which only looks better after it revealed its payment schedule for FY25.

What next?

The key point here is that the upside to IMBBY far outweighs any risks right now. The company expects positive performance in FY24, with robust growth in NGPs, which is crucial for the company's sustainability as traditional tobacco products fall out of favour.

Imperial Brands' dividend increase is also significant, increasing its forward yield appreciably and putting it above the average for the tobacco sector. Its share buybacks will also continue to benefit investors, besides potentially increasing its price.

In any case, going by its market multiples, there's still further upside to IMBBY. Not only is it still trading at a lower valuation compared to peers, its P/E is also lower than what it was even last year.

While there can be a plateauing in price as the stock markets get more bullish in Q4 2024, in line with historical trends, this won't be a reflection on the stock's fundamentals. I'm retaining a Buy rating on Imperial Brands.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
